← Bioregional Organizations

🟡 Tarun Bharat Sangh

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

A community-led water conservation organization restoring India's river systems through traditional knowledge and modern watershed management. TBS has revived 14 rivers, built thousands of water harvesting structures, and established a network of water stewards combining technical expertise with cultural revival.

Bioregional Context

Rajasthan is India's largest state by area and one of its most water-scarce regions, characterized by arid and semi-arid landscapes with minimal annual rainfall (200-400mm). The region's water crisis is exacerbated by depleting groundwater tables, desertification, and increasingly unpredictable monsoon patterns due to climate change.

Place
Rajasthan, India
links
https://www.canva.com/design/DAGZcco_p_s/SGmpZwnbMJKz-03C8c8GgQ/edit|https://www.ceew.in/sustainable-agriculture-initiatives/tarun-bharat-sangh-johad-water-conservation-harvesting-and-revitalisation

Tags

💧 Watershed_Repair ⛲ Water_Management 🔘 Governance_Bodies ♾️ Commons_Governance_Protocols 🎒 Learning_Programs 📣 Political_Advocacy 🔅 Traditional_Ecological_Knowledge 🪢 Network_Weaving 🛐 Cultural_and_Spiritual_Practices

Background


Rajasthan is India's largest state by area and one of its most water-scarce regions: arid and semi-arid country with typically 200–400mm of rain per year. Falling groundwater, desertification, and a more erratic monsoon deepen the crisis. Harvesting traditions that had sustained communities for generations were neglected in recent decades, contributing to farm decline and outmigration.

Historically, the Alwar region was water-rich into the early twentieth century. Communities practiced rainwater harvesting through johads—earthen check dams that hold runoff so it can soak in and recharge aquifers. By the 1980s scarcity was severe; parts of the region were “dark zones” in state irrigation records—groundwater critically low.

Land grew unsuitable for farming or herding, so many working-age men left for cities, leaving women and elders behind. Villages turned to Tarun Bharat Sangh after barren fields and collapsing livelihoods had already done deep harm.

Origins and Network


From 1985, Tarun Bharat Sangh (TBS), a Rajasthan-based NGO, focused on water conservation, beginning in Gopalpura in the Alwar area. The team stayed in the village to understand conditions. An elder described the old johad approach—still sound, but weakened by years without resources, backing, or confidence.

The first johad, chabootre wala johad, was built in 1985 to show the method still worked. When it held water and some farmers could grow bajra again, the team added mewalon ka baandh in 1986. Both structures helped refill wells, recharge groundwater, and restore fields.

Activities

🔸

Watershed Restoration and River Revival

 🔸

Cultural Revival for Ecological Kinship

 🔸

Community-Led Governance

 🔸

Water Literacy and Education

 🔸

Water Warriors

 🔸

National and International Advocacy

Vision and Legacy


The programme still prioritizes highly marginalized communities—currently including Scheduled Tribe and Scheduled Caste villages in Rajasthan’s Karauli district—showing how traditional knowledge, community ownership, and long commitment can move landscapes from dry wells toward flowing water and from despair toward hope.

TBS describes five decades of work with Rajasthan communities as helping revive 14 rivers, raise forest cover by 30%, return about 70% of once-barren land to cultivation, and protect five rivers from encroachment—bringing water security to over one million families. With water came more food security and livelihood; agriculture and milk production rose in 100% of project areas and farmer incomes doubled in participating villages, giving young people reason to stay or return.

As Rajendra Singh and Indira Khurana write in “Rejuvenation of Rivers”: “Change is possible. Through love and respect: Love for nature and respect for communities, and then nurturing of both. Through understanding the terrain – the endless recharge possibilities that the fractured rocks offer – and then healing the environment through appropriate water conservation structures and involving the people and their contribution. Lastly, through maintaining transparency.”
